nativeRange.habitat: Lagoons, reef flats, and seaward reefs; commonly found in harbors, among dead corals, and in small caves in protected inshore waters.

diet.notes: Generally offered large chunks of meaty foods two to three times per week, such as krill, silversides, raw shrimp, squid, clam, and mussel. Feeding tongs are strongly recommended to avoid accidental bites, as this species has poor eyesight and may strike at movement near the tank opening.

goodWith: large, robust fish that cannot fit in the eel's mouth

summary: A large Indo-Pacific moray eel reaching up to ~90 cm, generally considered manageable for experienced fish-only saltwater keepers who can provide a well-filtered, 500 L+ aquarium with extensive rockwork and a tightly sealed lid.
careNotes: Gymnothorax fimbriatus is a large, active moray that tends to explore its territory at night, making a tightly sealed, weighted lid essential — escape attempts are commonly reported. Sources describe the species as quite messy, producing significant waste, so robust mechanical filtration and an efficient protein skimmer are generally advised. Feeding is typically done with tongs 2–3 times per week; hand-feeding should be avoided as bites can cause serious bacterial infections requiring prompt medical attention. The species has poor eyesight and may strike at any movement near the tank opening. Although often sold as beginner-level, its large adult size (commonly 76–93 cm based on cross-source data), heavy bioload, and escape tendency make it better suited to keepers who have managed large predatory fish before. Compatible with large, robust fish that cannot be swallowed; shrimp, crabs, and other small invertebrates are likely to be eaten. The species is noted to be 'with caution' reef safe — it generally ignores corals but will consume any crustaceans or small fish that share the tank. FishBase notes this species may be a protogynous hermaphrodite, though this has not been confirmed definitively in captivity. Two sources disagreed on minimum tank size: BlueZoo Aquatics listed 50 gallons (~189 L) while LiveAquaria listed 125 gallons (~473 L); given the species' documented maximum length of up to 93 cm (FishBase) and its active nature, the larger figure was selected and rounded up to 500 L. Temperature sources also diverged — LiveAquaria recommended 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C) while FishBase reported a wild preferred range of 24.9–29°C; the values used here represent an average of the two sources.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-checked sizing, temperament, diet, and reef-safe status. BlueZoo listed a smaller minimum tank (50 gal vs LiveAquaria's 125 gal) and rated temperament as 'aggressive' vs LiveAquaria's 'semi-aggressive'; mid-point values were used per playbook averaging rules.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy, native range, wild adult size (max 93.4 cm TL), and temperature range cross-check. FishBase reports a higher preferred temperature range (24.9–29°C) than LiveAquaria's hobbyist recommendation (22.2–25.6°C); averaged values used.
